title: Inequalities & Absolute Value
blurb: Compound inequalities (and/or), absolute-value equations, and absolute-value inequalities — distances on the number line.

lessons.title: Multi-Step Inequalities
lessons.concreteComparison: A freelancer charges a \$40 setup fee plus \$25 an hour and wants each job to earn MORE than \$190. That's $25h + 40 > 190$ — an inequality with the full equation pipeline inside it: distribute, herd, divide. Everything transfers, with one alarm bell you already know: divide by a negative and the symbol flips.
lessons.objective: Solve inequalities requiring distribution and variables on both sides, and express solutions on the number line.
concept: Multi-step inequalities run the SAME pipeline as equations: distribute, herd variables, isolate — the $<, >, \le, \ge$ just rides along.
The flip rule survives intact from Grade 7: multiplying or dividing both sides by a NEGATIVE reverses the symbol. Adding, subtracting, or positive-scaling never flips.
Solutions are rays: draw them with an open circle (strict) or closed circle (inclusive) and a shaded direction.
Test one number from your ray in the ORIGINAL inequality — a solved ray that fails its own test always means a missed flip.
lessons.keyIdea: Equation pipeline + the flip rule = every linear inequality; verify with a test point.

facts.title: The pipeline carries over
facts.explanation: The inequality symbol rides along through every legal move.

facts.title: Flip only on negative scaling
facts.explanation: Adding and subtracting never flip; only ×/÷ by negatives do.

workedExamples.solution: $25h > 150 \to h > 6$. Jobs longer than $6$ hours qualify.

commonMistakes.text: Flipping when subtracting a variable term.
commonMistakes.correction: Moving $5x$ across by subtraction never flips the symbol — only MULTIPLYING or DIVIDING by a negative does.

commonMistakes.text: Shading toward the variable's side of the symbol.
commonMistakes.correction: Read the solved form: $x < -4$ shades LEFT of −4. When unsure, test a point instead of trusting the arrow's direction.

steps.title: One flip, one trigger
beats: Trigger: multiplying or dividing both sides by a NEGATIVE.
Herding variables never flips. Scaling by negatives always does.

steps.title: The test point
beats: Pick an easy number from your answer ray.
True → ray confirmed. False → you missed a flip.

steps.title: Flip or not?
steps.explanation: Add $4x$: $12 \le 6x \to x \ge 2$. No flip — we never scaled by a negative.

steps.title: Rays, earned properly
steps.intro: Pipeline through; test a point at the end.

examples.prompt: A moving van rents for \$60 plus \$0.80 per km, on a \$140 budget. How far can you drive?

steps.title: Solve and shade
steps.intro: Track the symbol at every line.

problems.prompt: Which value is in the solution set of $9 - x > 2x$?

steps.title: Budgets are inequalities with lawyers
steps.body: Engineering specs are written almost entirely in inequalities: a bridge cable must hold AT LEAST five times its working load, a processor must stay UNDER 100°C, an aircraft part must weigh NO MORE THAN its budget. Design engineers speak of 'margins' — how far inside the inequality you are. Equations describe the world; inequalities keep it safe.

steps.title: What you learned
points: The full equation pipeline works on inequalities — the symbol rides along.
Flip ONLY when multiplying/dividing by a negative.
Seal every answer with a test point in the original.

lessons.title: Compound Inequalities
lessons.concreteComparison: A rollercoaster sign: riders must be at least $120$ cm AND at most $195$ cm. A parking rule: free for under $30$ minutes OR after $18{:}00$. Real-world conditions come in pairs, joined by two tiny words — and the words matter. AND traps you between two fences; OR opens two separate gates.
lessons.objective: Solve AND (between) and OR (either side) compound inequalities and graph their solution sets.
concept: An **AND** compound like $-2 < x \le 3$ demands BOTH conditions: the solution is the overlap — a segment between two fences.
Sandwich form solves all three parts at once: $-5 \le 2x + 1 < 7$ → subtract 1 everywhere, divide by 2 everywhere: $-3 \le x < 3$.
An **OR** compound like $x < -1$ or $x > 4$ accepts EITHER condition: the solution is two rays pointing away from each other.
Graphs tell the story instantly: AND = one segment (possibly empty!); OR = two rays (possibly everything!). Always sketch.
Watch for impossible ANDs ($x < 1$ and $x > 5$: nothing) and trivial ORs ($x < 5$ or $x > 1$: everything).
lessons.keyIdea: AND = overlap (a segment); OR = union (two rays). Sandwiches solve three parts at once.

facts.title: AND = between
facts.explanation: Both fences at once — the overlap segment.

facts.title: OR = either
facts.explanation: Two rays; membership in either one admits you.

workedExamples.solution: Left: $x < -2$. Right: $x > 3$. Solution: $x < -2$ or $x > 3$ — two outward rays.

workedExamples.statement: A coaster requires height $h$ with $120 \le h \le 195$ (cm). Is a $118$ cm child admitted? A $195$ cm adult?
workedExamples.solution: $118 < 120$ — no. $195 \le 195$ — yes, exactly at the fence, and $\le$ includes it.

commonMistakes.text: Operating on only two of a sandwich's three parts.
commonMistakes.correction: $-5 \le 2x + 1 < 7$: the −1 and ÷2 hit ALL THREE zones, or the sandwich falls apart.

commonMistakes.text: Writing an OR answer as a sandwich.
commonMistakes.correction: x < −2 or x > 3 is NOT '−2 > x > 3' (nonsense) — two outward rays can't be written as one between-statement.

steps.title: AND traps, OR opens
beats: $x < -1$ or $x > 4$: two rays fleeing the middle.

steps.title: Three zones, one operation
beats: (Divide a sandwich by a negative? All symbols flip — and the sandwich reverses.)

steps.title: Read the graph
steps.explanation: No number is both above 4 AND below 2 — the fences don't overlap.

steps.title: Fences and gates
steps.intro: Solve each part; then overlap or unite.

examples.prompt: Normal human body temperature is roughly $36.1 \le T \le 37.2$ (°C). Express a FEVER as an inequality.

problems.explanation: Both fences point the same way — the stricter one ($\ge 3$) rules.

problems.prompt: A thermostat keeps $19 \le T \le 23$. Which temperature triggers the system?
problems.explanation: $18.5 < 19$ falls outside the band; $21$ and $23$ are inside (23 is exactly on the closed fence).

steps.title: The Goldilocks zone
steps.body: Astronomers hunting habitable planets solve a giant AND: close enough to the star for liquid water AND far enough not to boil — a ring-shaped 'Goldilocks zone' that is literally a compound inequality in orbital radius. Earth sits comfortably inside; Venus fails the left fence, Mars scrapes the right. Every exoplanet headline you've read was a sandwich inequality wearing a telescope.

steps.title: What you learned
points: AND = overlap segment; OR = union of rays.
Sandwiches: one operation, all three zones.
Sketch always — empty ANDs and everything-ORs hide in the algebra.

lessons.title: Absolute-Value Equations
lessons.concreteComparison: A bottling machine targets $500$ ml but tolerates being off by exactly $4$ ml before rejecting. Which volumes sit EXACTLY at the limit? The ones $4$ away from $500$: both $496$ and $504$. 'Distance from the target equals 4' is $|v - 500| = 4$ — and distance questions always have TWO answers, one on each side.
lessons.objective: Solve absolute-value equations by splitting into two cases, checking for extraneous cases and no-solution traps.
concept: $|X| = k$ (with $k > 0$) means X sits $k$ away from zero — two spots: $X = k$ or $X = -k$. Every absolute-value equation splits into two ordinary ones.
ISOLATE the absolute value first: $2|x - 1| + 3 = 11 \to |x - 1| = 4$, THEN split into $x - 1 = 4$ or $x - 1 = -4$.
$|X| = 0$ has exactly one solution ($X = 0$), and $|X| = -5$ has NONE — distance can't be negative. Check the right side before splitting.
Read $|x - a|$ as 'distance between $x$ and $a$' — the geometry makes every equation a sentence: $|v - 500| = 4$ says 'v is 4 away from 500.'
lessons.keyIdea: Isolate the bars, check the right side's sign, then split: $X = k$ or $X = -k$.

facts.title: Two cases
facts.explanation: Distance k from zero happens twice (k > 0).

facts.title: Impossible equations
facts.explanation: Absolute values never go negative — check before splitting.

workedExamples.solution: An absolute value can't equal a negative number. NO solution — no algebra required.

commonMistakes.correction: $2|x - 1| + 3 = 11$: dividing the ±11 into cases first is wrong. Strip the 2 and the 3 to get |x − 1| = 4, THEN split.

commonMistakes.text: Negating the whole equation instead of the right side.
commonMistakes.correction: The second case of $|x - 1| = 4$ is $x - 1 = -4$ — the INSIDE equals the negative — not $-(x-1) = -4$, which just repeats case one.

steps.title: Distance has two homes
steps.teach: Slide along the line and watch the distance-from-zero readout: every positive distance is achieved at TWO spots, one left, one right. That symmetry is the whole theory of absolute-value equations.

steps.title: Isolate first, sanity-check the sign
beats: Strip everything OUTSIDE the bars first.
Distance is never negative — read the right side before working.

steps.title: Count the solutions
steps.explanation: Distance zero means $4x - 8 = 0$ exactly: $x = 2$, the only point AT the target.

examples.prompt: A part is acceptable when its width $w$ satisfies $|w - 25| = 0.5$ AT the tolerance limit. Find the limit widths.

steps.title: Two, one, or none
steps.intro: Isolate the bars before anything else.

problems.explanation: Two points sit 2 away from 6: $4$ and $8$.

steps.title: Tolerance is a two-sided promise
steps.body: Machine shops literally stamp absolute-value equations on blueprints: '25 ± 0.5 mm' IS $|w - 25| \le 0.5$ in engineering shorthand. A part can fail by being too big OR too small — two cases, just like your algebra. The ± notation predates the |x| notation by centuries; manufacturing was solving both cases long before math class named them.

steps.title: What you learned
points: $|X| = k$ splits into $X = k$ and $X = -k$ (two spots at distance k).
Isolate the bars first; $|X| = $ negative means no solution.
Read $|x - a| = k$ as 'x is k away from a' — geometry first.

lessons.title: Absolute-Value Inequalities
lessons.concreteComparison: The bottling machine ACCEPTS volumes within $4$ ml of $500$: $|v - 500| \le 4$ — that's a BAND from $496$ to $504$. Quality control REJECTS volumes more than $4$ away: $|v - 500| > 4$ — everything OUTSIDE the band, two tails. Less-than hugs the target; greater-than flees it. Two pictures, and every absolute-value inequality is one of them.
lessons.objective: Solve |X| < k (between) and |X| > k (outside) inequalities and connect them to compound inequalities.
concept: $|X| < k$ means 'within $k$ of zero': the segment $-k < X < k$. It UNPACKS into an AND sandwich.
$|X| > k$ means 'farther than $k$ from zero': two tails, $X < -k$ or $X > k$. It unpacks into an OR.
Memory hook: less-th**AN**d (|X| < k → AND/between); great**OR** (|X| > k → OR/outside).
Same discipline as equations: isolate the absolute value FIRST, and mind the impossible cases — $|X| < -2$ has no solution; $|X| > -2$ is true for EVERYTHING.
$|x - a| < k$ reads 'x is within k of a' — the interval centered at $a$ with radius $k$: $(a - k, \; a + k)$.
lessons.keyIdea: Less-than → between (AND); greater-than → outside (OR). Isolate first; centers and radii do the rest.

facts.title: Within
facts.explanation: A band around the center — an AND.

facts.title: Beyond
facts.explanation: Two tails fleeing the center — an OR.

commonMistakes.correction: Greater-than means OUTSIDE: two tails ($X < -k$ or $X > k$), never $-k < X < k$ — that's the less-than picture.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting to flip the second tail's inequality.
commonMistakes.correction: $|2x - 3| > 5$: the second case is $2x - 3 < -5$ — the symbol REVERSES with the negated side.

steps.title: Center ± radius
beats: $|x - a| < k$: within $k$ of the center $a$.
The bars are a compass drawing an interval.

steps.title: Band or tails?
steps.explanation: At least 1 away from 3: outside the band — two tails from 2 and 4.

examples.prompt: A thermostat is 'in range' when $|T - 21| < 2$ (°C). Express the range, and classify $T = 23$.
steps: Exactly on the fence — out of range (strict).

steps.title: Unpack the bars
steps.intro: AND for <, OR for > — after isolating.

problems.prompt: Which inequality describes 'x is within 0.5 of 7'?
problems.explanation: Distance from 7 (the center) at most 0.5 (the radius).

steps.title: GPS lives inside a band
steps.body: Your phone's GPS reading is really a band inequality: 'you are within 5 meters of here' is $|\text{position} - \text{estimate}| < 5$ in two dimensions — a disk. Surveyors, missile engineers, and delivery apps all speak in 'error radius.' When the app's blue circle shrinks, the k in your inequality just got smaller.

steps.title: What you learned
points: $|x - a| < k$ is the interval centered at $a$ with radius $k$.

lessons.title: Modeling with Inequalities
lessons.concreteComparison: A delivery drone can carry at most $2.5$ kg. A student needs an exam average of at least $85$ across four tests. A machine part passes if it's within $0.3$ mm of spec. Three stories, three tools — plain, compound, absolute-value — and choosing the RIGHT inequality is the actual skill. The solving is yesterday's work.
lessons.objective: Translate constraints into plain, compound, or absolute-value inequalities and interpret solution sets in context.
concept: Phrase dictionary, extended: 'at most' → $\le$, 'at least' → $\ge$, 'between a and b' → sandwich, 'within k of a' → $|x - a| \le k$, 'differs from a by more than k' → $|x - a| > k$.
Averages hide inequalities: 'average of four tests at least 85' means $\frac{s_1 + s_2 + s_3 + s_4}{4} \ge 85$ — multiply through and solve for the unknown test.
Real-world sets get trimmed: counts are whole numbers, weights are nonnegative, and a 'solution' outside physical limits is a red flag to re-read the story.
Answer the question in the story's language: 'she needs at least a 94' beats '$s \ge 94$'.
lessons.keyIdea: Match the phrase to the tool — plain, sandwich, or bars — then solve with yesterday's machinery.

facts.title: Extended dictionary
facts.explanation: The phrase names the inequality type before any algebra.

facts.title: Averages unpack
facts.explanation: Multiply through by the count to free the unknown.

workedExamples.statement: Riya scored $82, 88, 79$ on three tests. What must she score on the fourth for an average of at least $85$?
A drone carries at most $2.5$ kg and already holds a $0.7$ kg camera. How many $0.3$ kg sensor packs can it add?
A part passes inspection when its length is within $0.3$ mm of $120$ mm. Write and solve the inequality.

commonMistakes.text: Turning 'within' into a plain one-sided inequality.
commonMistakes.correction: 'Within 0.3 of 120' bounds BOTH sides — it's |L − 120| ≤ 0.3, not L ≤ 120.3 alone.
The unknown test belongs INSIDE the average: (249 + s)/4, not 249/3 compared to 85.

tryIt.statement: Tickets cost \$14 and a group has at most \$100 after paying \$16 for parking. How many tickets can they buy?
A recipe stays safe when the oven is within $15°$ of $180°$C. Express the safe band.

steps.title: Three inequalities, three jobs
beats: The story's PHRASE picks the tool.

steps.title: Trim to the possible
beats: A negative 'solution' usually means a set-up slip.
End in the story's units and words.

steps.title: Pick the tool
steps.prompt: 'The pool's pH must stay within $0.4$ of $7.2$.' Which model?
steps.explanation: 'Within' bounds both directions — the bars capture both fences at once ($6.8 \le p \le 7.6$).

examples.prompt: A phone plan includes $10$ GB; each extra GB costs \$3. Keep the bill's extras under \$20 — how many extra GB?
An elevator (limit $630$ kg) carries a $70$ kg operator. How many $80$ kg passengers may ride?
steps: Seven passengers, exactly at the limit.

examples.prompt: Bowling scores $145$ and $172$; what third score keeps the average between $150$ and $170$ inclusive?

steps.title: Model the constraint
steps.intro: Tool first, algebra second, story-answer last.

problems.prompt: A charity needs at least \$500; it has \$140 and sells bracelets at \$8. How many must it sell?
A package ships free when its weight is between $1$ and $5$ kg inclusive. Which weight does NOT qualify?
problems.explanation: $0.8 < 1$ misses the band; the fences at 1 and 5 are included.

problems.prompt: A dart lands 'in the ring' when its distance from center differs from $10$ cm by less than $1$ cm. Which model?
problems.explanation: 'Differs from 10 by less than 1' is a band: $9 < d < 11$.

steps.title: The GPA rescue equation
steps.body: Every student who has ever computed 'what do I need on the final?' was solving an average inequality — usually at midnight. Professors report it's the single most-practiced piece of algebra in adult life. The formula never changes: required total minus points banked. Now you can do it BEFORE midnight.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Phrases choose tools: limits → plain; between → sandwich; within → bars.
Averages unpack by multiplying through by the count.
Trim to whole numbers and physical sense; answer in story language.

lessons.title: Inequalities in Action
lessons.concreteComparison: A start-up sells phone cases at \$15 that cost \$6 to make, with \$1{,}800 monthly fixed costs. 'When are we profitable?' is an inequality. 'Keep defects within 2% of the 5% target' is another. This capstone runs the whole toolkit — plain, compound, absolute value, and the modeling instincts — on the problems businesses actually solve.
lessons.objective: Combine all inequality types to analyze profit, constraints, and tolerance problems.
concept: **Profit**: income > costs. With price $p$, unit cost $c$, fixed costs $F$: profitable when $pn > cn + F$, i.e. $n > \frac{F}{p - c}$ — the break-even count, crossed.
**Feasibility**: several constraints must hold at once — an AND of inequalities. The solution is whatever survives every fence.
**Tolerance**: quality targets are absolute-value bands around a spec; tightening quality means shrinking $k$.
Boundaries are decision points: solving the matching EQUATION finds the fence; the inequality tells you which side of it you want to live on.
lessons.keyIdea: Break-even = the fence; profit = the side you want. Stack constraints with AND; quality is a shrinking band.

facts.title: Break-even, crossed
facts.explanation: Fixed costs divided by per-unit margin — profit lives past it.

facts.title: Feasible = survives all fences
facts.explanation: Each requirement trims the set; what remains is feasible.

workedExamples.statement: Cases sell at \$15, cost \$6 to make, fixed costs \$1{,}800/month. How many sales make a profitable month?
A budget requires: venue \$400, food \$12 per guest, total at most \$1{,}000, and at least $30$ guests. Feasible guest counts?
A factory targets a $5\%$ defect rate, acceptable within $2$ percentage points. Express the acceptable band.

commonMistakes.correction: AT n = 200, income EQUALS costs — profit is zero. Profit starts strictly past the fence: n > 200.

commonMistakes.text: Checking constraints one at a time and keeping partial answers.
commonMistakes.correction: Feasibility is an AND: a guest count of 55 passes the minimum but busts the budget. Every fence must hold simultaneously.

tryIt.statement: A tutor charges \$30/hour with \$240 of monthly software costs. Hours needed for a profitable month?
Feasible study plan: at least $2$ h of math, at most $6$ h total, and $1.5$ h of science. How much math is possible?

steps.title: The fence and the good side
beats: Margin per unit ($9$) divides the fixed load ($1800$).

steps.title: Stack the fences
beats: Add a fence, shrink the set.
Optimization (Grade 12+) hunts the BEST point inside.

steps.title: Bands that shrink
beats: Tighter band → better product, harder maths.
The band IS the quality standard, written in algebra.

steps.title: Cross the fence
steps.prompt: Lemonade: \$2 per cup, \$0.50 ingredients per cup, \$18 stand rental. Profitable from which cup?
steps.explanation: $1.5n > 18 \to n > 12$: cup 12 breaks even; cup 13 earns.

examples.prompt: A gym's monthly costs are \$5{,}400; memberships are \$45. Members needed to profit?
A truck route pays \$0.60/km minus \$90/day fuel-and-fees, and regulations cap driving at $600$ km/day. Profitable AND legal range?
A juice bottle's label says $330$ ml, legal when within $2\%$ of the label. Express and solve.

steps.title: Business hours
steps.intro: Choose fences deliberately; report the good side.

problems.prompt: Bake sale: cupcakes \$3 each, ingredients \$0.75 each, table fee \$27. Profit needs…
Constraints: $x \ge 4$, $x \le 9$, and $x \ne 6$… which value is feasible?
problems.explanation: $7$ passes all three fences; $6$ is excluded, $10$ busts the cap.

problems.prompt: A resistor is usable within $5\%$ of $200$ ohms. Which reading FAILS?

steps.title: The million-dollar comma
steps.body: Contracts are inequalities in prose, and the difference between 'at least' and 'more than' — one unit — has decided real lawsuits over bonuses, shipping loads, and election recounts. Lawyers bill hundreds an hour to argue about whether a fence is open or closed. You now settle the same question with a circle: filled or hollow.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Break-even is the fence; profit is strictly beyond it.
Feasibility stacks fences with AND — every constraint must hold.
Quality specs are absolute-value bands; tightening quality shrinks k.

practice.statement: Test scores $78, 91, 84$: what fourth score gives an average of at least $85$?

testYourself.solution: Subtract 4: $-5 < -x \le 2$. Multiply by −1 and REVERSE everything: $5 > x \ge -2$, i.e. $-2 \le x < 5$.

testYourself.statement: Solve $|5 - x| < 3$ and describe the graph.
testYourself.solution: $-3 < 5 - x < 3 \to 2 < x < 8$: open band from 2 to 8.
(a) none; (b) exactly one ($x = 0$); (c) all real numbers — distance always beats a negative.

testYourself.statement: A courier van (limit $1{,}200$ kg) already holds $250$ kg. Boxes weigh $38$ kg. How many boxes fit, and does the answer change at a $1{,}190$ kg limit?
testYourself.solution: $38b + 250 \le 1200 \to b \le 25$. At $1190$: $38b \le 940 \to b \le 24.7$ — so $24$: yes, one fewer box.
